course_description:
This course equips you with the knowledge and skills you will need to undertake academic research in the broad area of communication. It explores the paradigmatic assumptions and theoretical perspectives behind the many different ways of conducting research in communication, and by extension, the broad area of humanities and social sciences where communication plays a central role. We provide practical examples and guidance as to how communication research should be planned and implemented.

COMU7292 equips you with the ability to critically evaluate researchﾠinformation and professionally design academic research projects. The course covers knowledge and skills in both qualitative and quantitative research design, including literature review, research question formation, choice of appropriate methods, data collection, data analysis, and research ethics. Various practical examples are used to illustrate how to apply research knowledge and skills in designing valid research projects to examine authentic research problems that you can relate to. This course builds a solid foundation for you to progress to the thesis courses later in your postgraduate program of study. Going beyond the university, this course builds your capability to be a critical consumer and knowledgeable producer of research information.

from your

with:

Your student ID
The course code
A list of three tutorial preferences (in order of preference)
Reason for the change – e.g. timetable clash, elite athlete status, SAP
Teaching staff do not have access to change tutorials or help with timetables; all timetabling changes must be processed through the Timetabling Team.
course_aims: COMU7292 aims to provide you with essentialﾠknowledge and skillsﾠfor designing and conducting academic research. The course covers various theoretical, methodological, cultural, and ethical issues that youﾠneed to consider when designing and conductingﾠa research project. You will learn how to searchﾠand critically reviewﾠliterature, form research questions, design and conduct a study, and analyse data in a valid manner to address your research questions. The course also enhances your cultural sensitivity and ethicalﾠawareness when you researchﾠinﾠdiverse cultural contexts.

learning_outcomes.description: Build a solid knowledge base of the theoretical assumptions underlying different approaches to research.
Critically review literature relevant to your project and use it to form a conceptual framework for your project.
Apply knowledge and skills to design a valid research project and choose appropriate methods to address your project aims and research questions.
Evaluate cultural and ethical issues related to research processes and design strategies to properly address them.
Use clear and concise language suitable to the conventions of academic writing in presenting your research project.

assessment_details.title: Problem Statement & Research Plan
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment 1 (Problem Statement and Research Plan) aims to get you started on selecting a research topic and plan for your project. You are encouraged to select a research problem for your project that you can relate to. This facilitates your utilisation of your prior knowledge and experiences. You should frame the problem into a researchable one and outline your plan to address it. You need to cite at least 10 academic references from academic journals or books. In addition, you may cite from non-academic sources (e.g., government reports, internet sources), where necessary. Please do not cite Wikipedia as academic references for your research project. The required referencing style for your in-text citation and references list at the end of your assessment is APA 7th edition referencing style. The UQ Library website has APA 7th edition referencing style guide, which also has information on how to cite non-English references, lecture notes, and power points. The word limit (1000 words) is suggestive. No penalties will apply based on the sole reason that your assessment is above or under the suggested word limit. More details of this assessment will be provided in class sessions and on Blackboard. Note: This assessment is to be submitted via Turnitin. Submission of assessment to the course coordinator or your tutor as email attachment will NOT be accepted. Your assessment is evaluated according to the marking criteria specified in this course, NOT evaluated with reference to the achievement of other students or your performance in other courses. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit via TurnItIn on Blackboard.
TurnItIn Receipts:
This assignment will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using TurnItIn . Before submitting any assignments for this course, you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
When you successfully submit your assessment to TurnItIn you will see text confirming your submission is complete, before being redirected to your Assignment inbox. On this page you can:
· View the name of the submitted file
· View date and time of the upload
· Resubmit your paper (if necessary)
· Download your submitted paper
· Download digital receipt.
If you cannot see your submission in your Assignment inbox you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
· Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot).
· Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again.
· If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your TurnItIn assignment .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: In-Class Tests (5 tests)
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment 2 consists of 5 in-class tests to be conducted during tutorial sessions over 5 weeks, starting in Week 4 and ending in Week 9 (inclusive). No in-class test is scheduled in Week 6 because Friday of Week 6 is Good Friday Public holiday. Each in-class test weighs 6 marks. The duration of each test is approximately 30 minutes. Electronic devices, readings, and written notes are not permitted during the test. Tutors will provide hard copies of test worksheet to students in their tutorial session of the week, and students submit their completed test worksheet to their tutor at the end of the test time in the tutorial session. In--class tests are in the format of short-answer questions. The question(s) of each test are based on the learning materials of a specific week including lecture notes and power points, tutorial notes and power points, and required readings that are available on Blackboard. More details of in-class tests will be provided in class sessions and on Blackboard course site. Note: Each completed in-class test worksheet is to be submitted in the tutorial session in which the test is conducted. Submission of a completed in-class test worksheet to the course coordinator or tutor as email attachment will NOT be accepted. Your assessment is evaluated according to the marking criteria specified in this course, NOT assessed with reference to the achievement of other students or your performance in other courses. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit in tutorial sessions.
Each completed in-class test worksheet is to be submitted in the tutorial session in which the test is conducted. Submission of a completed in-class test worksheet to the course coordinator or tutor as email attachment will NOT be accepted.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Extension or deferral for this assessment is not available because each in-class test is conducted and completed in person in a tutorial session. If you are unable to take an in-class test in a specific week due to illness or exceptional circumstances, please contact your tutor or the course coordinator via email and attach your supporting documents. If your application for taking the make-up test is approved, your tutor will arrange a time and venue for you to take the make-up test.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
No late submission is permitted because this is in-class and in-person assessment. Please refer to the Extension or Deferral availability notes below if you are unable to take an in-class test on the day of your tutorial in a specific week.

assessment_details.title: Research Proposal
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment (Research proposal) aims to assess your ability to be a critical consumer and a knowledgeable producer of research. Your research proposal consists of three sections : 1) Introduction to the project, which states the purpose and significance of your study. 2) Brief literature review and research questions. Your literature review should be a concise and critical synthesis of previous research that functions to form a conceptual framework for your project. Your research questions should be informed by your literature review and be answerable if your proposed research project is to be carried out. 3) Methodology, which explains the design of your study, the specific methods chosen appropriate to the research topic and research questions, data collection strategies, and data analysis techniques. You are required to justify decisions made at each step of the research process. You also need to explain how cultural and ethical issues, if applicable, are addressed. This final assessment is expected to be informed by and developed from Assessment 1. However, you are allowed to work on a project for Assessment 3, which is different from that of your Assessment 1, if you wish to. You should cite at least 20 academic references from academic journals or books as scholarly support. You may include the academic references cited for Assessment 1 and they count towards the minimum 20 academic references required for Assessment 3. In addition, you may cite from non-academic sources, where necessary. Please do not cite Wikipedia as academic references for your project. The required referencing style for your in-text citation and the references list at the end of your assessment is APA 7th edition referencing style . The UQ Library website has APA 7th edition style guide, which also has information on how to cite non-English references, lecture notes and power points. The word limit (2000) is suggestive. No penalties will apply based on the sole reason that your assessment goes above or under the suggested word limit. Further information on this assessment will be provided in class sessions and on Blackboard. Note: This assessment is to be submitted via Turnitin. Submission of assessment to the course coordinator or tutor as email attachment will NOT be accepted. Your assessment is evaluated according to the marking criteria specified in this course, NOT assessed with reference to the achievement of other students or your performance in other courses. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Week 1 Lecture: Introduction to the Course This first lecture provides an overview of the course structure, learning objectives, activities, requirements, assessments and other important course related information. Learning outcomes: L01
Week 1 Tutorials: No Tutorials Tutorials start in Week 2.

Week 2 Lecture: Turning Ideas into Research Projects This lecture discusses how to turn ideas into research as well as identifies various challenges that you may encounter when planning for a research project. Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04
Week 2 Tutorial: Choosing a Research Topic This tutorial discusses what you need to consider when choosing your research topic area. Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04

Week 3 Lecture: Building Conceptual Framework This lecture focuses on searching for literature, critically reviewing previous studies, and developing research questions for your project. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 3 Tutorial: Refining your Research Topic Area This tutorial discusses refining your project area including what you can do if you find too many or two few academic references in your research topic area. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 4 Lecture: Understanding Mixed Methods Design This lecture explores how qualitative and quantitative approaches can complement each other in mixed methods design. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Week 4 Tutorial: Enhancing Trustworthiness of your Research This tutorial discusses how you can enhance the trustworthiness of your research design and findings. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 5 Lecture: Knowing Research Ethics This lecture introduces ethical principles for conducting academic research, including ethical issues related to data collection, data analysis, data storage, and data reporting. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 5 Tutorial: Applying Ethical Principles in Research This tutorial discusses the ethical issues you need to consider in designing and conducting your research project. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 6 Lecture: Researching on People - Survey This lecture introduces survey methods in collecting data from people and discusses common challenges for researchers when they use survey methods in their research. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 6 Tutorial: No Tutorials this week No tutorials this week because Friday is a public holiday.
Mid-Semester Break Mid-Semester Break. No lecture and no tutorials. Have a nice Mid-Semester Break!

Week 7 Lecture: Researching on People - Interview This lecture introducing interview methods and discusses challenges that researchers encounter when using interview methods to collect data from people. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Week 7 Tutorial: Collecting Data using Interview This tutorial discusses various issues that you may need to consider when you collect data using interview method. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 8 Lecture: Researching on Texts This lecture focuses on discussing methods that researchers apply to collect and analyse data in words. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 8 Tutorial: Managing Data in Words This tutorial discusses various issues related to managing data in the form of words. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 9 Lecture: Researching on Images This lecture focuses on methods used to study images including how images can complement data in the form of words. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 9 Tutorial: Collecting Data from Images This tutorial discusses issues related to using images as research data. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Week 10 Lecture: Self-Directed Learning This week is self-directed learning. No lecture. You may use this time to get started with working on your final assessment. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Week 10 Tutorial: Self-Directed Learning This week is self-directed learning. No tutorials. You may use this time to get started with working on your Assessment. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 11 Lecture: Researching on the Internet This lecture discusses research on the internet, particularly social media websites. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 11 Tutorial: Collecting Digital Data This tutorial discusses how to address challenges of collecting data from online sources. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Week 12 Lecture: Putting the Research Proposal Together This final lecture discusses the structure and content of a research proposal as well as criteria for evaluating a research proposal. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 12 Tutorial: Questions and Answers This final tutorial is a Q&A drop-in session to address questions you may have about Assessment 3.
Week 13 Lecture: Self-Directed Learning This week is self-directed learning. No lecture. You may use this time to work on your Assessment 3. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 13 Tutorial: Self-Directed Learning This week is self-directed learning. No tutorial. You may use this time to work on your Assessment 3. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines Communication Expectations While you are a student at UQ, all communication must be conducted according to the UQ Student Code of Conduct . The UQ Library has a helpful Communicate and collaborate online module . Email is the primary way for you to send messages to, and receive information from, the School and our staff. You must use your UQ email address (not a private address) to communicate with staff. You should add a clear subject line, including course code, and a 2-3 word statement. You can send email at any time, however please do not expect responses outside normal working hours (Monday to Friday from ~8am to ~5pm). Emails that constitute bullying, harassment or discrimination against staff contravene the Student Code of Conduct. Emails like this will be reported to the University, and the matter will be pursued as misconduct. Learning activities
